era_context: The mid-14th century is characterized by the Hundred Years' War in its early phase, the Yuan Dynasty in advanced decline, the Ottoman Empire's consolidation in Anatolia, and the Black Death pandemic spreading westward from Central Asia (though not yet documented in Europe). This year falls three years before the plague's documented arrival in the Mediterranean and four years before the Battle of Crécy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (plague's spread is poorly documented in surviving Western sources)

events.title: Black Death pandemic spreads through Central Asia and the Islamic world
events.region: Central Asia, Islamic world, Mongol territories
events.description: The plague pandemic continues its westward spread through Central Asian trade routes and Mongol-controlled territories. By 1343, the disease is documented in regions controlled by the Golden Horde and other Mongol successor states. The exact trajectory and timing of the plague's spread are difficult to establish from surviving sources, but contemporary accounts suggest significant mortality in Central Asia and the Islamic world during the early 1340s. The plague's presence in the Caucasus and the Crimea is documented by the mid-1340s.
key_figures: Merchants and traders along the Silk Road

sources.name: Later Islamic and Chinese sources describing plague in Central Asia
Paleogenomic evidence from mass graves in Central Asia

events.certainty_note: The plague's presence in Central Asia by 1343 is probable based on the trajectory of the pandemic and later accounts, but precise dating is uncertain. Contemporary sources from Central Asia are sparse.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English-French tensions and military buildup
events.description: Edward III of England and Philip VI of France continue their conflict over the French throne and territorial disputes. English forces are building alliances with Low Countries powers and preparing for major military campaigns. The English are consolidating their naval power in the English Channel. By 1343, the stage is being set for the major campaigns of 1346 and beyond. Contemporary sources note English military mobilization and French defensive measures.

sources.name: English royal records and military accounts

events.certainty_note: English-French tensions and military activity in 1343 are attested in royal records, though specific operations are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty experiences continued internal instability
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under Toghon Temür experiences continued internal instability. Regional warlords, Mongol princes, and Han Chinese officials compete for power. Corruption, inflation, and administrative paralysis are widespread. Peasant unrest and bandit movements are increasing. The central government's authority is weakening. By 1343, the dynasty's decline is evident to contemporary observers.
key_figures: Various regional warlords and Mongol princes

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Yuan instability in the 1340s is well-documented in official records.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates power in northwestern Anatolia
events.description: Orhan I continues to consolidate Ottoman power in northwestern Anatolia. The Ottoman state is expanding its territory, strengthening its military organization, and establishing administrative structures. By 1343, the Ottomans are the dominant power in the region. Contemporary sources note Ottoman military strength and territorial expansion.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion under Orhan I is well-attested, though specific events in 1343 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Pope Clement VI elected and begins papacy
events.description: Pierre Roger is elected Pope Clement VI in May 1342 (some sources date this to 1343). Clement VI (r. 1342–1352) is a French cardinal and a skilled diplomat. He consolidates papal authority and navigates complex political relationships with European monarchs. The papacy is based in Avignon (the Avignon papacy period, 1309–1377). Clement VI's papacy is marked by political maneuvering, ecclesiastical authority, and (from 1346 onward) the challenge of responding to the Black Death pandemic.
events.certainty_note: Clement VI's election and the beginning of his papacy are well-documented in papal records. The date is sometimes given as May 1342 or 1343, depending on the source.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of the plague's spread through Central Asia in 1343 is uncertain. Some sources suggest the plague was already present in Central Asia by the early 1340s, while others place its arrival later. The extent of plague mortality in Central Asia in 1343 is difficult to quantify; later accounts may conflate events from multiple years. The significance of English-French tensions in 1343 is sometimes overstated; the major English campaigns did not begin until 1346. The date of Clement VI's election is sometimes given as May 1342 rather than 1343, depending on the source.
historiographic_note: The year 1343 is moderately well-documented for Europe and the Mediterranean, with good sources for English-French military activity and papal politics. However, the plague's spread through Central Asia is poorly documented in surviving Western sources. Chinese sources are available but often compiled retrospectively. Ottoman sources are later compilations. The historiography of 1343 is shaped by the dramatic events of 1346 (Crécy, plague arrival in Europe), which may have overshadowed other significant developments in 1343. The year 1343 is often treated as a prelude to 1346 rather than as a significant year in its own right.

graph_edges.note: Plague spread in Central Asia in 1343 continues westward in 1344
English-French tensions in 1343 culminate in Battle of Crécy in 1346
Clement VI's papacy begins in 1343; he will respond to Black Death from 1346 onward
